blocks:
Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦٓ أَنْ أَنذِرْ قَوْمَكَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١)
Indeed We sent Noah to his people, [saying,] ‘Warn your people before a painful punishment overtakes them.’ (1)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنِّى لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢)
He said, ‘O my people! Indeed, I am a manifest warner to you. (2)
أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ وَأَطِيعُونِ (٣)
Worship Allah and be wary of Him, and obey me, (3)
يَغْفِرْ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ إِنَّ أَجَلَ ٱللَّهِ إِذَا جَآءَ لَا يُؤَخَّرُ ۖ لَوْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٤)
that He may forgive you some of your sins and respite you until a specified time. Indeed when Allah’s [appointed] time comes, it cannot be deferred, if you know.’ (4)

Content of Surah Nuh

This Surah, as is evident from its name, recounts the story of the Prophet Nuh عليه السلام. The life of this great Prophet has been mentioned in various Surahs of the Holy Quran, including Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Al-Mu'minun, Al-A'raf, and Al-Anbiya. The most detailed account is found in Surah Hud, which devotes approximately 25 verses to this Arch-Prophet (Ulul-'Azm) (from verse 25 to 49).

However, what appears in Surah Nuh is a specific part of his life that is not presented in this style elsewhere. This section pertains to his continuous and persistent invitation toward Monotheism (Tawhid), the nature and elements of this invitation, and the intricate details employed in this significant matter—all in the face of a stubborn, self-centered, and arrogant people who were absolutely unwilling to bow before the Truth.

Given that this Surah was revealed in Makkah, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the few Muslims of that time were in conditions similar to those of Nuh and his companions, it teaches them many lessons. One of the objectives of mentioning this account was precisely this, including:

1- It teaches them how to propagate to enemies through logical reasoning combined with complete love and compassion, utilizing every useful and effective means in this path.

2- It teaches them never to grow weary in the path of calling toward Allah, even if it takes many years and the enemy continues to sabotage.

3- It teaches them to hold the means of encouragement in one hand and the factors of warning (Inzar) in the other, utilizing both in the path of invitation.

4- The final verses of this Surah serve as a warning to the stubborn polytheists that if they do not submit to the Truth and do not yield to Allah's command, a painful end awaits them.

5- In addition to these, this Surah serves as a source of consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the early believers, and similar individuals, encouraging them to remain heartened by Allah's grace in their programs and to be patient and steadfast in the face of difficulties and hardships.

In other words, this Surah is a depiction of the permanent struggle between the supporters of Truth and Falsehood, and the programs that the supporters of Truth must employ on their path.

Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah Nuh will be among the believers whom the prayer of Prophet Nuh reaches." [^1]

And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day and reads His Book should not abandon the recitation of the Surah 'Inna Arsalna Nuhan' (Surah Nuh). For any servant who recites it seeking Allah's pleasure and with patience, whether in an obligatory or recommended prayer, Allah will settle him in the dwellings of the righteous and grant him three gardens of Paradise along with his own garden as an honor from Allah." [^1]

It goes without saying that the purpose of reciting it is to draw inspiration from the way and method of this great Prophet and the steadfastness and patience of his companions in the path of calling toward the Truth, and to place oneself within the radiance of his invitation—not a recitation devoid of reflection and thought, nor a thought devoid of action.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 359.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 359.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِ أَنْ أَنْذِرْ قَوْمَكَ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ (1) قالَ يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ (2) أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَ اتَّقُوهُ وَ أَطِيعُونِ (3) يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَ يُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى إِنَّ أَجَلَ اللَّهِ إِذا جاءَ لا يُؤَخَّرُ لَوْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (4)

Commentary

The First Message of Nuh

We mentioned that this Surah expresses that part of Nuh's life related to the matter of his invitation, teaching interesting points regarding the call toward the Truth, especially in the face of stubborn nations, to all wayfarers of Allah's path.

It begins first with the matter of his appointment (Ba'thah), stating: "Indeed, We sent Nuh to his people, [saying], 'Warn your people before there comes to them a painful punishment.'" (إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِ أَنْ أَنْذِرْ قَوْمَكَ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

This painful punishment could be the punishment of this world or the punishment of the Hereafter, and it is most appropriate that it refers to both, although by the context of the final verses of the Surah, the punishment of this world is primarily intended.

The emphasis on "Inzar" (warning), despite the fact that Prophets were both "warners" and "bringers of glad tidings," is because warning often has a stronger impact, just as the enforcement of laws throughout the world relies on warning and punishment.

Nuh, this "Ulul-'Azm" Prophet who was the bearer of the first Divine Law (Shari'ah) and creed and had a global mission, came to his people after receiving this command and said:

"O my people, indeed I am to you a clear warner." (قالَ يا قَوْمِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ)

"The goal is that you worship Allah alone and cast away whatever is other than Him, be mindful of Him (Taqwa), and obey my commands, which are the commands of Allah." (أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَ اتَّقُوهُ وَ أَطِيعُونِ) In reality, Nuh summarized the content of his invitation in three sentences: the worship of the One God, the observance of Taqwa, and obedience to the laws and commands he brought from Allah, which encompassed the totality of beliefs, ethics, and rulings.

Then he proceeds to encourage them, stating the important results of responding to this invitation in two short sentences, saying: If you respond to my call, "He will forgive you of your sins" (يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ). [^1] In fact, the famous rule "Islam erases what preceded it" is a law that has existed in all Divine and Monotheistic religions and is not exclusive to Islam.

Then he adds: "and delay you until a specified term," meaning He will lengthen your lives and keep the punishment away from you (وَ يُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى).

"Indeed, the term of Allah, when it comes, will not be delayed, if you only knew." (إِنَّ أَجَلَ اللَّهِ إِذا جاءَ لا يُؤَخَّرُ لَوْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

From this verse, it is clearly understood that the "Ajal" (term) and the end of a human's life are of two types: the "Appointed Term" (Ajal Musamma) and the "Final Term," or in other words, the "Lesser Term" and the "Greater Term," or the "Suspended Term" (conditional) and the "Definite Term" (absolute). The first type is a deadline that is subject to change and alteration; due to a person's wrong actions, it may be greatly advanced—Divine punishments being one such cause—and conversely, due to Taqwa, righteousness, and prudence, it may be greatly delayed.

However, the final term and deadline is by no means subject to change. This subject can be clarified with an example: a human being does not have the capacity for eternal life; even if all body systems function well, eventually a time comes when, due to extreme wear and tear, the heart stops on its own. However, observing health principles and timely prevention of diseases can lengthen a person's life, while neglecting these matters may shorten it significantly and bring it to an early end. [^1]

Key Point: Spiritual Factors in the Lengthening and Shortening of Life

Another point clearly derived from this verse is the influence of sins on the shortening of life, for it says: If you believe and practice Taqwa, Allah will grant you a long life and delay your death. Considering that sins always strike terrible blows to the human body or soul, understanding this meaning is easy.

In Islamic narrations, this meaning has also been heavily emphasized. For instance, in a profound Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Those who die due to sins are more than those who die due to their [natural] terms, and those who live due to righteousness are more than those who live due to their [natural] lifespans!" [^2]

[^1]: "Min" in this sentence is extra (za'idah) and for emphasis, because faith in Allah causes the forgiveness of all past sins—of course, those related to the rights of people (Haqq al-Nas) are separate, but in terms of the sin and the ruling of prohibition, forgiveness includes them as well. The possibility suggested by some commentators (like Fakhr al-Razi in al-Tafsir al-Kabir and Allameh Tabataba'i in al-Mizan) that "min" is partitive (tab'idiyyah) and refers to past sins but not future ones seems unlikely, as future sins are not the subject of the verse.

[^1]: We have had another discussion regarding the "Final Term" and "Suspended Term" under verse 2 of Surah Al-An'am (Vol. 5, p. 148).

[^2]: "Safinat al-Bihar," Vol. 1, p. 488, under the root "Dhanab."

Ayahs

قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى دَعَوْتُ قَوْمِى لَيْلًا وَنَهَارًا (٥)
He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed, I have summoned my people night and day (5)
فَلَمْ يَزِدْهُمْ دُعَآءِىٓ إِلَّا فِرَارًا (٦)
but my summons only increases their evasion. (6)
وَإِنِّى كُلَّمَا دَعَوْتُهُمْ لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ جَعَلُوٓا۟ أَصَـٰبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَٱسْتَغْشَوْا۟ ثِيَابَهُمْ وَأَصَرُّوا۟ وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ ٱسْتِكْبَارًا (٧)
Indeed whenever I have summoned them, so that You might forgive them, they would put their fingers into their ears and draw their cloaks over their heads, and they were persistent [in their unfaith], and disdainful in [their] arrogance. (7)
ثُمَّ إِنِّى دَعَوْتُهُمْ جِهَارًا (٨)
Again I summoned them aloud, (8)
ثُمَّ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَنتُ لَهُمْ وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْرَارًا (٩)
and again appealed to them publicly and confided with them privately, (9)

Commentary

I Used Every Opportunity to Guide Them, But...

In these verses, following the account of Noah’s mission to invite his people, words are quoted from his own tongue as he takes his complaint to the presence of his Lord—words that are deeply instructive.

Noah’s discourse in this regard provides a roadmap for all religious propagators. He says: "Noah said: 'My Lord! Indeed, I have invited my people night and day'" (قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُ قَوْمِي لَيْلًا وَنَهَارًا), and I did not falter for a single moment in guiding and preaching to them.

"But my invitation and guidance added nothing to them except flight [from the truth]" (فَلَمْ يَزِدْهُمْ دُعَائِي إِلَّا فِرَارًا). It is indeed strange that an invitation toward something would cause flight from it. However, considering that the effectiveness of invitations requires a degree of readiness, affinity, and mutual attraction, it is not surprising that it produces a reverse and negative effect in unready hearts. In other words, when stubborn individuals and enemies of the truth hear the call of the men of God, they exhibit resistance, and this very resistance distances them further from Allah and solidifies their disbelief and hypocrisy.

This is exactly like what is mentioned in verse 82 of Surah Al-Isra:

"And We send down of the Quran that which is a healing and a mercy for the believers, but it does not increase the wrongdoers except in loss" (وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ مَا هُوَ شِفَاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا). And the reason we read in the verses of the Quran that this Heavenly Book is "a guidance for the God-fearing" (هُدًى لِلْمُتَّقِينَ) [Al-Baqarah: 2] is that there must be a level of piety—however weak—within a human being for them to be ready to accept the truth. This stage is the "spirit of truth-seeking" and the readiness to accept truthful speech.

Then Noah continues his speech, adding: "O Allah! Whenever I invited them to believe so that You might grant them Your forgiveness, they put their fingers in their ears, wrapped themselves in their garments, persisted in their opposition and lack of faith, and became exceedingly arrogant" (وَإِنِّي كُلَّمَا دَعَوْتُهُمْ لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ جَعَلُوا أَصَابِعَهُمْ فِي آذَانِهِمْ وَاسْتَغْشَوْا ثِيَابَهُمْ وَأَصَرُّوا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا اسْتِكْبَارًا).

Putting fingers in the ears was so they would not hear the voice of truth. Wrapping themselves in their garments either means they threw their clothes over their heads to act as a support for the fingers pressed into their ears so that not even the slightest sound wave would reach their eardrums—and thus no message would be transmitted to the brain—or they wanted to cover their faces lest their eyes fall upon the celestial countenance of Noah, this great Prophet. In reality, they insisted that both the ear be closed to hearing and the eye to seeing! It is truly astonishing that a human can reach such a stage of enmity and hostility toward the truth that they do not even allow themselves to see, hear, or think.

In some Islamic commentaries, it is mentioned that some of those deviant people would take their children by the hand to Noah and say to their child:

"Beware of this man lest he misguide you; this is the testament my father gave to me, and I now give the same advice to you (so that I have fulfilled the right of testament and goodwill)." [1] This verse shows that Noah, with his long life spanning several generations, continued his divine invitation and never grew weary.

Furthermore, it shows that one of the major factors in their wretchedness was arrogance and pride, for they saw themselves as too superior to submit to a human like themselves—even if he was a representative of Allah and his heart was a center of knowledge, wisdom, and piety. This pride and arrogance has always been one of the major obstacles on the path of truth, and we observe its bitter fruit throughout human history in the lives of faithless individuals.

Noah continues his words in the presence of the Lord, saying: "O Allah! Then I invited them openly to monotheism and Your worship" (ثُمَّ إِنِّي دَعَوْتُهُمْ جِهَارًا).

In public gatherings and with a loud voice, I called them toward faith.

I was not content with even this: "I explained the reality of monotheism and faith to them both publicly and in private" (ثُمَّ إِنِّي أَعْلَنْتُ لَهُمْ وَأَسْرَرْتُ لَهُمْ إِسْرَارًا). According to some commentators, to influence this stubborn and selfish population, Noah followed his invitation through three different methods: sometimes only a private invitation,

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 361.

which was met with the fourfold reactions (putting fingers in ears, wrapping themselves in garments, persisting in disbelief, and acting arrogantly); sometimes only a public and open invitation; and sometimes he used a method of mixing public and private invitations, but none of these proved effective. [1] Fundamentally, the human constitution is such that if one advances so far on the path of falsehood that the roots of corruption become firm within him, penetrating the depths of his being and becoming a second nature, neither the invitation of the men of God will affect him, nor will the eloquent messages of Allah be of any use.

Key Points

1. The Method and Manner of Preaching

What is mentioned in the above verses regarding Noah’s invitation, while being a means of consolation for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the few believers who joined him in Mecca, presents a general and universal program for all preachers on the path of Allah.

He never expected people to answer his call by gathering in a public city center, where he could deliver the divine message in peace while everyone looked to his mouth and listened to his words. Rather, it is understood from the tone of the verses (and mentioned in some narrations) that he would sometimes go to people in their homes, or call them privately in the streets and markets, preaching with patience and a loving tone. At other times, he would come to public gatherings held for other purposes—such as celebrations or mourning—and with a loud

[1]: Tafsir al-Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 30, p. 136.

and clear voice, he would recite Allah’s command to them. Yet, he was always met with undesirable reactions, insults, and mockery, and sometimes physical assault; despite this, he never abandoned his work.

This extraordinary patience, that even more extraordinary compassion, and his peerless perseverance and steadfastness were his capital on the path of inviting to the true religion.

Most astonishingly, during his nine hundred and fifty years of invitation, only about eighty people believed in him. If we divide these two numbers, it becomes clear that for the guidance of each single person, he preached for an average of about twelve years!

If Islamic preachers possessed such steadfastness and perseverance, would not Islam, with its rich and attractive content, become world-encompassing?

2. Why Flee from the Truth?

Sometimes one wonders: is it possible for there to be people under this sky who are not even willing to hear the word of truth and flee from it? We are not talking about acceptance; we are talking merely about hearing.

But history says such individuals have been many.

Not only did the people of Noah put their fingers in their ears and wrap garments over their heads and faces when he invited them to monotheism so as not to hear or see the truth, but during the era of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), according to the explicit text of the Quran, there was a group who, when the soul-stirring voice of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) rose with the verses of the Quran, would create such a commotion with noise, shouting, and whistling that no one could hear his voice:

"And those who disbelieve say: 'Do not listen to this Quran and make noise during its recitation that you may overcome'" (وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَا تَسْمَعُوا لِهَذَا الْقُرْآنِ وَالْغَوْا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَغْلِبُونَ) [Fussilat: 26].

In the bloody history of Karbala, it is also recorded that when Imam Hussain (peace be upon him), the Master of Martyrs, wanted to guide and awaken the deviant enemies, they raised such a clamor that the Imam’s voice was lost among them. [1] Today, this program continues, albeit in other forms and guises. The partisans of falsehood, through various unhealthy entertainments, destructive music, narcotics, and the like, have created such an atmosphere that people—especially the youth—cannot hear the melodious voice of the men of God.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 45, p. 8.

Ayahs

فَقُلْتُ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفَّارًا (١٠)
telling [them]: ‘‘Plead to your Lord for forgiveness. Indeed, He is all-forgiving. (10)
يُرْسِلِ ٱلسَّمَآءَ عَلَيْكُم مِّدْرَارًا (١١)
He will send for you abundant rains from the sky, (11)
وَيُمْدِدْكُم بِأَمْوَٰلٍ وَبَنِينَ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ أَنْهَـٰرًا (١٢)
and aid you with wealth and sons, and provide you with gardens and provide you with streams. (12)
مَّا لَكُمْ لَا تَرْجُونَ لِلَّهِ وَقَارًا (١٣)
What is the matter with you that you do not look upon Allah with veneration, (13)
وَقَدْ خَلَقَكُمْ أَطْوَارًا (١٤)
though He has created you in [various] stages? (14)

Commentary

The Worldly Reward of Faith

In continuation of his influential discourse to guide that stubborn and rebellious people, Nuh (peace be upon him) this time relies on glad tidings and encouragement. He gives them a firm promise that if they repent from polytheism and sin, Allah will open the doors of His mercy to them from every direction. He says: "I told them: Ask forgiveness from your Lord, for He is Oft-Forgiving" (فَقُلْتُ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفَّاراً).

Not only will He purify you from sins, but "if you do so, He will send [rain from] the sky upon you in abundance" (يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً) 1. In short, both the rain of spiritual mercy and the rain of material blessing will encompass you. It is noteworthy that he says: "He sends the sky upon you," meaning it will rain so much that it is as if the sky itself is descending! However, since it is the rain of mercy, it brings neither destruction nor harm; rather, it is the source of lushness, greenery, and freshness everywhere.

Then he adds: "And provide you with increase in wealth and children," (وَ يُمْدِدْكُمْ بِأَمْوالٍ وَ بَنِينَ‌). "And provide for you gardens and provide for you rivers." (وَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ وَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ أَنْهاراً).

In this manner, he promised them one great spiritual blessing and five great material blessings. The great spiritual blessing is the forgiveness of sins and being purified from the pollution of disbelief and disobedience.

1: "Midrar" is derived from the root "darr" (rhyming with jarr), which originally means the flowing of milk from a mother's breast, and was subsequently used for the falling of rain. "Midrar" is an intensive form (Sighat al-Mubalaghah).

As for the material blessings: the falling of beneficial, timely, and abundant rains; the increase of wealth; the increase of children (human capital); fruitful gardens; and flowing rivers. Indeed, faith and piety, according to the testimony of the Holy Quran, cause the prosperity of both this world and the Hereafter.

It is mentioned in some narrations that when this stubborn people refused to accept Nuh's invitation, drought and famine overtook them. Many of their properties and children perished, their women became barren and rarely gave birth. Nuh said to them: "If you believe, all these calamities and afflictions will be repelled from you," but they paid him no heed and continued to show persistence until the final punishment arrived and harvested them all!

Then, once again, he returns to warning and says: "What is the matter with you that you do not fear Allah and do not attribute majesty to Him?" (ما لَكُمْ لا تَرْجُونَ لِلَّهِ وَقاراً) 1

"While He has created you in stages" (وَ قَدْ خَلَقَكُمْ أَطْواراً). First, you were a worthless "drop of fluid" (Nutfah); before long, He turned you into a "clinging clot" (Alaqah), and after that into a "lump of flesh" (Mudghah). Then He gave you human form and limbs.

Then He clothed your frame with the garment of life and gave you soul, sense, and movement. In this way, you passed through the various embryonic stages one after another until you were born from the mother as a complete human being. Again, the stages of life and different forms of living continued. You are always under His Lordship (Rububiyyah) and are constantly being renewed and finding a new creation. How is it that you do not bow your head in submission before the majestic threshold of your Creator?

Not only do you take on different forms physically, but the face of your soul and spirit is also constantly changing. Each of you has a talent, and in every head there is a taste, and in every heart there is a love. All of you are constantly being transformed; the feelings of childhood give their place to the feelings of youth, and those in turn give way to the feelings of middle age and old age.

Thus, He is with you everywhere and guides you at every step. Despite all this grace and favor from Him, why all this ingratitude and disrespect?

Key Point: The Relationship Between "Piety" and "Prosperity"

From various verses of the Quran, including the verses above, this point is well-derived: that faith and justice are the cause of the prosperity of societies, while disbelief, oppression, and sin are the cause of destruction.

In verse 96 of Surah Al-A'raf, we read: "And if the people of the cities had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth."

And in verse 41 of Surah Ar-Rum, we read: "Corruption has appeared throughout the land and sea by [reason of] what the hands of people have earned."

And in verse 30 of Surah Ash-Shura, it is stated: "And whatever affliction befalls you, it is because of what your hands have earned." And in verse 66 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, it is said: "And if only they had upheld [the law of] the Torah, the Gospel, and what was revealed to them from their Lord, they would have consumed [provision] from above them and from beneath their feet" (and the blessings of the earth and sky would have encompassed them), and other verses of this kind.

This "relationship" is not merely a spiritual one; rather, in addition to the spiritual relationship whose effects we clearly see, there is also a clear material relationship in this regard.

Disbelief and lack of faith are the source of a lack of sense of responsibility, law-breaking, and forgetting moral values. These matters cause the loss of social unity, the shaking of the foundations of trust and confidence, the wasting of human and economic resources, and the disruption of social balance.

It is obvious that a society governed by these matters will quickly retreat and take the path of decline and destruction.

And if we see societies that, despite a lack of faith and piety, enjoy relative progress in their material condition, we must attribute that to their relative observance of some moral principles, which are the heritage of previous prophets and the result of the efforts of divine leaders, scholars, and sages throughout the centuries.

In addition to the above verses, Islamic narrations also place great emphasis on this meaning—that seeking forgiveness (Istighfar) and abandoning sin cause an increase in sustenance and the improvement of life. For example:

In a Hadith from Ali عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said: "Seek forgiveness frequently to draw sustenance toward yourself." 1

In another Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said: "Whoever Allah has bestowed a blessing upon, let him praise Allah the Exalted; and whoever finds his sustenance delayed, let him seek forgiveness from Allah; and whoever is saddened by an affair, let him say: 'There is no power nor strength except by Allah.'" [2]

In Nahj al-Balagha, we also read: "Allah, the Sublime, has made seeking forgiveness a cause for the abundance of sustenance and mercy for the creation, for He, the Sublime, said: 'Ask forgiveness from your Lord, for He is Oft-Forgiving; He will send [rain from] the sky upon you in abundance...'" [3]

The truth is that the punishment for many sins consists of deprivations in this world, and when a person repents and takes the path of purity and piety, Allah removes these punishments from him. [4]

1 and [2]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 424. [3]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Sermon 143. [4]: We had another explanation in this regard under the title "Sin and the Destruction of Societies" following verse 52 of Surah Hud (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 9, p. 131).

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَوْا۟ كَيْفَ خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ طِبَاقًا (١٥)
Have you not seen how Allah has created the seven heavens in layers, (15)
وَجَعَلَ ٱلْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُورًا وَجَعَلَ ٱلشَّمْسَ سِرَاجًا (١٦)
and has made therein the moon for a light and the sun for a lamp? (16)
وَٱللَّهُ أَنۢبَتَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ نَبَاتًا (١٧)
Allah made you grow from the earth, with a [vegetable] growth. (17)
ثُمَّ يُعِيدُكُمْ فِيهَا وَيُخْرِجُكُمْ إِخْرَاجًا (١٨)
Then He makes you return to it, and He will bring you forth [without fail]. (18)
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ بِسَاطًا (١٩)
Allah has made the earth a vast expanse for you (19)
لِّتَسْلُكُوا۟ مِنْهَا سُبُلًا فِجَاجًا (٢٠)
so that you may travel over its spacious ways.’’ ’ (20)

Commentary

The Gardener of Existence Nurtured You Like a Flower

In his profound and reasoned discourses before the stubborn polytheists, Prophet Nuh (peace be upon him):

First, he took them by the hand and led them into the depths of their own being so they might witness the signs within themselves (as mentioned in the previous verses). Then, as the verses under discussion state, he invited them to study the signs of Allah in the macrocosm of creation and led them through a journey of the horizons [1]. He began first with the heavens, saying: "Do you not see how Allah has created the seven heavens one above another?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَوْا كَيْفَ خَلَقَ اللَّهُ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً) [2]. The word tibaqan is a verbal noun from the root mufa'alah meaning "correspondence." Sometimes it refers to one thing being placed above another, and sometimes it refers to the harmony and correspondence between two things; both meanings apply here.

According to the first meaning, the seven heavens are situated one above the other. As we have previously stated in the commentary regarding the seven heavens, one notable interpretation is that everything we see with the naked eye or through telescopes—including the fixed stars and planets—is all part of the first heaven. The other six worlds are situated one above the other beyond that, currently outside the reach of human science and knowledge, though it is possible that in the future, humanity may gain the merit to discover these wondrous and vast worlds one after another [1]. According to the second possibility, the Quran refers to the harmony and correspondence of the seven heavens in their order, grandeur, and beauty.

[1] There is a discussion among commentators as to whether these words are a continuation of Nuh's speech or independent sentences addressed by Allah to the Muslims as a parenthetical statement. However, many prefer that they are a continuation of Nuh's speech, and the apparent context of the verses necessitates this. If the phrase "And Nuh said" appears after these verses, it is because Nuh finished his speech to the people and turned toward Allah's threshold to complain about them. [2] Tibaqan can be an "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq) or a "circumstantial qualifier" (hal).

Then He adds: "And [He] made the moon therein a light, and made the sun a burning lamp" (وَ جَعَلَ الْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُوراً وَ جَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ سِراجاً). It is true that within the seven heavens there are millions upon millions of glowing stars that are far more radiant than our sun and moon, but what is important for us and affects our lives are this sun and moon of the solar system, which illuminate our living space—one during the days and the other during the nights [2]. The use of the term siraj (lamp) for the "sun" and nur (light) for the "moon" is because the light of the "sun" emanates from within itself like a lamp, whereas the light of the "moon" is not from within itself but is like a reflection from a mirror; therefore, the word nur, which has a more general concept, is used for it.

[1] We have discussed the various interpretations of the seven heavens in detail under verse 29 of Surah Al-Baqarah (Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh). [2] From this, it becomes clear that the pronoun fihinna (in them), which apparently refers back to the "seven heavens," creates no difficulty, because the discourse is about light and illumination for us. Therefore, there is no need to take fi to mean "with" or the pronoun hinna to mean "the lowest heaven" (reflect on this).

This difference in terminology is also seen in other verses of the Quran. We have provided more explanation in this regard under verse 5 of Surah Yunus (Volume 8, page 226).

Then He returns once more to the creation of man and adds: "And Allah has caused you to grow from the earth as a plant" (وَ اللَّهُ أَنْبَتَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ نَباتاً) [1]. The use of the term inbat (causing to grow) regarding man is because, firstly, the initial creation of man is from dust; secondly, all the nutrients man consumes and by which he grows and develops come from the earth—either directly like vegetables, grains, and fruits, or indirectly like animal meat; and thirdly, there is a great similarity between man and plants, and many of the laws governing the nutrition, reproduction, growth, and development of plants also govern man. This expression is very meaningful regarding man and shows that Allah's work in the matter of guidance is not merely the work of a teacher or instructor, but rather like the work of a gardener who places plant seeds in a favorable environment so that their latent potentials may bloom.

Regarding Lady Maryam (peace be upon her), we also read in verse 37 of Al-Imran: وَ أَنْبَتَها نَباتاً حسنا "And [He] caused her to grow with a goodly growth." These all point to that same subtle point.

[1] In this verse, grammatically it should have been inbatan, but the verse has an implied structure: "He caused you to grow from the earth, so you grew as a plant" (Tafsir Fakhr Razi and Abu al-Futuh Razi).

Next, He addresses the issue of the Resurrection, which was another complex issue for the polytheists, saying: "Then He will return you into it, and will bring you forth [again]" (ثُمَّ يُعِيدُكُمْ فِيها وَ يُخْرِجُكُمْ إِخْراجاً).

In the beginning you were dust, and once again you shall return to dust; and the same One who had the power to create you from dust in the beginning has the ability to clothe your stature in the garment of life once more after you have become dust.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 25; p. 78

The transition from the issue of "Monotheism" (Tawhid) to "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), which is reflected so beautifully in the above verses, expresses the close relationship between these two issues. In this way, Nuh argues against his opponents through the system of creation both for Monotheism and for the Resurrection.

He returns again to the signs of the horizons and the indicators of Monotheism in the macrocosm and speaks of the blessing of the earth's existence, saying: "And Allah has made the earth for you as a carpet spread out" (وَ اللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ بِساطاً) [1]. It is not so rough that you cannot rest or travel upon it, nor so soft that you sink into it and lack the power to move. It is not so hot and scorching that you suffer from its heat, nor so cold and heatless that living upon it becomes difficult for you. Furthermore, it is a spread-out carpet, prepared and containing all the necessities of your life.

[1] Bisat is from the root bast meaning to spread something out; therefore, the word bisat is applied to anything spread out, of which a carpet is one instance.

Not only are the level lands like a spread-out carpet, but even the mountains—due to the valleys and clefts that exist within them and are traversable—are also a spread-out carpet. "That you may go about therein in broad ways" (لِتَسْلُكُوا مِنْها سُبُلًا فِجاجاً).

Fijaj (rhyming with mizaj) is the plural of fajj (rhyming with hajj), meaning a valley located between two mountains, and it is also applied to wide roads [1]. In this way, Nuh, in this part of his speech, sometimes points to the signs of Allah in the heavens and celestial bodies, sometimes to His various blessings on the planet Earth, and sometimes to the structure of man himself and the issue of his life and existence—which is both a proof for knowing Allah and a proof for the issue of the Resurrection.

However, neither those initial warnings, nor the glad tidings and encouragements, nor the logical arguments had any effect on the dark hearts of this stubborn people. They continued in their opposition and disbelief and refused to accept the Truth, the end result of which stubbornness we shall see in the following verses.

[1] Mufradat of Raghib, root fajj.

Ayahs

قَالَ نُوحٌ رَّبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِى وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَن لَّمْ يَزِدْهُ مَالُهُۥ وَوَلَدُهُۥٓ إِلَّا خَسَارًا (٢١)
Noah said, ‘My Lord! They have disobeyed me, following someone whose wealth and children only add to his loss, (21)
وَمَكَرُوا۟ مَكْرًا كُبَّارًا (٢٢)
and they have devised an outrageous plot. (22)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَذَرُنَّ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ وَلَا تَذَرُنَّ وَدًّا وَلَا سُوَاعًا وَلَا يَغُوثَ وَيَعُوقَ وَنَسْرًا (٢٣)
They say, ‘‘Do not abandon your gods. Do not abandon Wadd, nor Suwa, nor Yaghuth, Ya‘uq and Nasr,’’ (23)
وَقَدْ أَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا ۖ وَلَا تَزِدِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا ضَلَـٰلًا (٢٤)
and already they have led many astray. Do not increase the wrongdoers in anything but error.’ (24)
مِّمَّا خَطِيٓـَٔـٰتِهِمْ أُغْرِقُوا۟ فَأُدْخِلُوا۟ نَارًا فَلَمْ يَجِدُوا۟ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَنصَارًا (٢٥)
They were drowned because of their iniquities, then made to enter a Fire, and they did not find any helpers for themselves besides Allah. (25)

Commentary

Divine Grace Deals Gently with You...

When Noah (عليه السلام) exerted his final efforts over the course of hundreds of years, and that people—except for a small group—continued to persist in disbelief, idol-worship, deviation, and corruption, he despaired of their guidance. He turned to the threshold of Allah and, within a reasoned supplication, requested punishment for them, as we read in the verses under discussion:

"Noah said: 'My Lord! Indeed, they have disobeyed me and followed him whose wealth and children have added nothing to him but loss'" (قالَ نُوحٌ رَبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِي وَ اتَّبَعُوا مَنْ لَمْ يَزِدْهُ مالُهُ وَ وَلَدُهُ إِلَّا خَساراً).

This refers to the fact that the leaders of this people were a group whose only distinction was abundant wealth and children—wealth and children that were utilized only in the path of corruption. They neither served the creation nor showed humility before the Creator, and these vast resources became the source of their pride, rebellion, and defiance.

If we look at human history, we see that many leaders of various nations have been of this same cloth: people whose only distinction was amassing forbidden wealth and producing unrighteous children, followed by defiance and rebellion, and ultimately imposing their thoughts upon the oppressed masses and shackling them.

Then he adds: "And these [misleading and misled] leaders devised a tremendous plot" (وَ مَكَرُوا مَكْراً كُبَّاراً).

"Kubbār" (كُبَّار), which is an intensive form of "Kubr" (greatness) and is mentioned here in the indefinite form, indicates that they had devised vast and grand satanic plans to mislead the people and prevent them from accepting Noah's call. But what were these plans?

It is not precisely clear.

It is possible that it was the issue of idol-worship itself, for according to some narrations, idol-worship had no precedent before Noah; rather, the people of Noah originated it. The matter began when, in the interval between Adam and Noah, there were righteous men to whom the people showed great affection. Satan (and satanic humans) exploited the people's affection and encouraged them to build statues of those great figures and to honor those statues.

However, it was not long before subsequent generations forgot the historical context of this matter and imagined that these statues were venerable beings that must be worshipped. Thus, they became occupied with the worship of idols, and the oppressive aristocrats, by deceiving them through this means, brought them into bondage, and the "tremendous plot" took place.

The next verse can serve as evidence for this point, because after the cryptic reference to this great plot, it adds: "Their leaders said: 'Do not abandon your gods and your idols'" (وَ قالُوا لا تَذَرُنَّ آلِهَتَكُمْ‌). And never accept Noah's call to the One God—a God who is never seen and cannot be touched by hand!

Specifically, they emphasized five idols and said: "Never leave the idols 'Wadd', 'Suwa’', 'Yaghuth', 'Ya’uq', and 'Nasr', and do not let go of them!" (وَ لا تَذَرُنَّ وَدًّا وَ لا سُواعاً وَ لا يَغُوثَ وَ يَعُوقَ وَ نَسْراً).

From the context, it is understood that these five idols possessed special distinctions and were the focus of particular attention for that misguided people; for this reason, their opportunistic leaders also emphasized their worship.

Regarding where these five idols originated, there are various narrations:

  1. Some have said these are the names of five righteous men who lived before Noah. When they passed away, statues of them were built as memorials at the instigation of Iblis and were honored, and gradually this turned into idol-worship.

  2. Some have said these are the names of five children of Adam; whenever one passed away, his statue was built for remembrance, but after some time these matters were forgotten and in the era of Noah, the wave of their worship surged.

  3. Others believe these are the names of idols built during the era of Noah himself. This was because Noah prevented the people from circumambulating the grave of Adam. A group, instigated by Iblis, built statues instead and became occupied with worshipping them [1]. Coincidentally, these five idols were passed down to the pre-Islamic Arabs, and each tribe chose one of these idols for themselves. Of course, it is very unlikely that the physical idols themselves were transferred; rather, it appears that their names were transferred and idols were built with these names. However, some commentators have narrated from Ibn Abbas that these five idols...

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, and other commentaries (under the verses discussed).

...were buried during the Flood of Noah, and in the era of Arab Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), Satan dug them out and invited the people to worship them [1]. Again, there is discussion regarding how these idols were distributed among the pre-Islamic Arab tribes.

Some have said the idol "Wadd" belonged to the "Bani Kalb" tribe in the land of "Dumat al-Jandal" (a city near Tabuk which is called "Jowf" today), and "Suwa’" belonged to the "Hudhayl" tribe in the land of "Ruhat", and the idol "Yaghuth" belonged to the "Bani Qutayf" or "Bani Madhhij" tribe, and "Ya’uq" to the "Hamdan" tribe, and "Nasr" to the "Dhu al-Kala’" tribe of the Himyar tribes [2]. Altogether, of these five idols, three (Yaghuth, Ya’uq, and Nasr) were located in the land of Yemen, which disappeared with the dominance of "Dhu Nuwas" over Yemen as the people of that region converted to Judaism [3]. The famous historian "Waqidi" says: "The idol Wadd was in the shape of a 'man', Suwa’ in the form of a 'woman', Yaghuth in the form of a 'lion', Ya’uq in the form of a 'horse', and Nasr in the form of a 'vulture' (the well-known bird)" [4]. Of course, the pre-Islamic Arabs, especially the inhabitants of Mecca, had other idols as well, including the idol "Hubal," which was considered their greatest idol and was placed inside the Kaaba, being 18 cubits in length! And the idol "Isaf" was opposite the Black Stone, and the idol "Na'ila" was opposite the Yemenite Corner (the southern corner of the Kaaba), as well as the idols "Lat" and "Uzza" [5].

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6787. [2] & [3]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 364 and A'lam al-Qur'an, p. 631. [4] & [5]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 364.

Then Noah adds: "O Allah, these [misleading and selfish] leaders have led many astray" (وَ قَدْ أَضَلُّوا كَثِيراً) [1]. "O Allah! Increase the wrongdoers in nothing but misguidance" (وَ لا تَزِدِ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا ضَلالًا). The meaning of increasing the misguidance of the wrongdoers and oppressors is the withdrawal of Divine success (tawfiq) from them, which leads to their wretchedness, or it is the punishment they receive for their oppression, whereby Allah takes the light of faith from them and replaces it with the darkness of disbelief.

Or, it is the inherent property of their actions that is attributed to Allah, because every effect produced by any being is by His command (reflect on this).

Whatever the case may be, it does not contradict Allah's wisdom regarding the issue of faith, disbelief, guidance, and misguidance, nor does it result in the removal of free will.

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, Allah speaks the final word in this regard: "Because of their sins they were drowned and then made to enter the Fire, and they found for themselves no helpers besides Allah [to defend them against His wrath]"

[1]: The pronoun in "adallu" (أضلوا) refers to the wealthy chiefs and leaders of this group, based on the evidence of the previous verse which says: "And they said: 'Do not abandon your gods...'" However, some commentators have suggested the pronoun refers to the "idols," as they were the cause of misguidance (similar to what appears in Ibrahim:36, though not as a masculine plural pronoun but as a feminine plural). But this possibility seems very unlikely in the verse under discussion.

(مِمَّا خَطِيئاتِهِمْ أُغْرِقُوا فَأُدْخِلُوا ناراً فَلَمْ يَجِدُوا لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَنْصاراً) [1]. The expression of the verse indicates that after being drowned, they immediately entered the Fire, and it is remarkable that they should enter Fire immediately from water! This fire is the fire of Purgatory (Barzakh), for according to the testimony of Quranic verses, a group is punished in the realm of Barzakh after death, and according to some narrations, the "grave" is either a garden from the gardens of Paradise or a pit from the pits of Hell! The possibility has also been raised that it refers to the Fire of Resurrection, but since the occurrence of the Resurrection is certain and not far off, it is mentioned in the past tense [2]. Some have also suggested it refers to a fire in this world, saying that by Allah's command, a fire appeared amidst the waves of the Flood and swallowed them up! [3]

[1]: "Min" (مِن) in "mimma khati'atihim" (مِمَّا خَطِيئاتِهِمْ) denotes causality (sababiyyah) or reasoning (ta'lil), and "ma" (ما) here is extra (za'idah) for emphasis. [2]: Fakhr al-Razi has mentioned this as one opinion in his commentary, Vol. 30, p. 145. [3]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 11, p. 280.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ نُوحٌ رَّبِّ لَا تَذَرْ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ دَيَّارًا (٢٦)
And Noah said, ‘My Lord! ‘Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless. (26)
إِنَّكَ إِن تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا۟ عِبَادَكَ وَلَا يَلِدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا فَاجِرًا كَفَّارًا (٢٧)
If You leave them, they will lead astray Your servants, and will beget none except vicious ingrates. (27)
رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَىَّ وَلِمَن دَخَلَ بَيْتِىَ مُؤْمِنًا وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَلَا تَزِدِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا تَبَارًۢا (٢٨)
My Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and whoever enters my house in faith, and the faithful men and women, and do not increase the wrongdoers in anything but ruin.’ (28)

Commentary

This Corrupt and Corrupting People Must Go!

These verses continue the words of Nuh and his complaint regarding his people to the presence of Allah, as well as his curse upon them. He says:

"And Nuh said: 'My Lord! Leave not upon the earth a single inhabitant from among the disbelievers!'" (وَ قالَ نُوحٌ رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً).

He uttered these words when he had become completely despaired of their guidance, having exerted his final efforts and endeavors to bring them to faith without result, as only a small group had believed in him.

The expression "upon the earth" (عَلَى الْأَرْضِ) indicates that both Nuh's mission was universal and the subsequent flood and punishment were global.

"Dayyar" (on the pattern of Sayyar) is derived from the root "Dar" (abode), meaning someone who dwells in a house. This word is usually used in general negations; for example, it is said: "There is not a dayyar in the house" (meaning there is not a single inhabitant in the house). ^1

Then, Nuh provides the reasoning for his curse and adds: "For if You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and they will beget none but wicked disbelievers!" (إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ وَ لا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً).

This shows that the curses of the Prophets, including Nuh عليه السلام, were not out of anger, rage, vengeance, or malice, but were based on a logical calculation. Nuh was not like impatient individuals who lose their temper over the slightest thing and resort to cursing; rather, he opened his lips in curse only after nine hundred and fifty years of invitation, patience, endurance, and suffering, and after reaching a state of total despair.

As for how Nuh knew that they would never believe, and furthermore, that they would mislead the servants of Allah in that environment, and beyond that—

^1: Some have said it was originally "Diwar" (on the pattern of Hayawan), then the "Waw" changed to "Ya" and was merged into it, becoming "Dayyar" (Al-Bayan fi Ghara'ib al-Quran, Vol. 2, p. 465, and Tafsir Fakhr Razi under the verses in question).

—that their future generation would also be corrupt and corrupting?! Some have said this was through knowledge of the Unseen (Ghayb) granted to him by Allah. It is also possible that Nuh derived this from divine revelation, where He says: "And it was revealed to Nuh: 'None of your people will believe except those who have already believed'" (Surah Hud, Verse 36). ^1 However, it is also plausible that Nuh reached this reality through natural means and ordinary calculations; for a people who are preached to for nine hundred and fifty years with the most effective expressions and yet do not believe, there is no hope for their guidance. Since this group of disbelievers held the overwhelming majority in society and had all resources at their disposal, they would naturally strive to seduce others, and the future generations of such a people would certainly be corrupt. It is also possible to combine these three possibilities.

"Fajir" refers to one who commits a vile and heinous sin, and "Kaffar" is an intensive form of "Kufr" (disbelief). Thus, the difference between these two terms is that one relates to practical aspects (actions) and the other to belief (creed).

From the sum of these verses, it is clearly understood that divine punishments are based on "Wisdom." A population that is corrupt and corrupting, and whose future generations are also at risk of corruption and deviation, has no right to life according to the Wisdom of Allah. A flood, a lightning bolt, an earthquake, or another calamity descends and wipes them from the face of the earth, just as the Flood of Nuh washed the earth clean of the filth of that wicked people's existence.

And considering that this divine law is not restricted to any specific time or place, we must be mindful—

^1: This meaning has also been mentioned in several narrations (refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 428).

—that if today a people were corrupt and corrupting, and their children were "wicked" and "disbelievers," they should expect divine punishment, for there is no discrimination in these matters; it is a Divine Sunnah (Law).

The expression "they will lead Your servants astray" (يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ) may refer to the small group of believers who had faith in Nuh during that long period, or it may refer to the oppressed masses of people who follow the religion of their misguided leaders due to pressure.

In conclusion, Nuh prays for himself and those who believed in him as follows: "My Lord! Forgive me, and my parents, and whoever enters my house as a believer, and all believing men and believing women, and increase the wrongdoers in nothing but destruction" (رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَ لِوالِدَيَّ وَ لِمَنْ دَخَلَ بَيْتِيَ مُؤْمِناً وَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ وَ لا تَزِدِ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا تَباراً). ^1 This plea for forgiveness is because Nuh wants to say that although he carried out continuous preaching for hundreds of years and endured every kind of torture and suffering in this path, since it is possible that a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) might have occurred on his part during this time, he requests pardon for that as well, and never considers himself exonerated before Your Holy Presence.

Such is the state of the "Awliya Allah" (Friends of Allah), who, after all that toil and effort in the way of Allah, still consider themselves falling short and never fall into the trap of pride or self-importance—

—unlike shallow individuals who, by performing a small deed, become so proud as if Allah were indebted to them! Nuh, in reality, seeks forgiveness for several groups:

  1. First, for himself, lest any shortcoming or tark al-awla had occurred.
  2. Second, for his parents, as a gesture of appreciation and recognition of their efforts.
  3. Third, for all those who believed in him, however few they were, and subsequently boarded the Ark with him, as that Ark was also the "house" of Nuh.
  4. Fourth, for the believing men and women throughout the world and throughout history, thereby establishing his connection with believers across the entire globe.

But at the end, he again emphasizes the destruction of the wrongdoers, indicating that they deserved such punishment because of their oppression.

Key Points

Nuh (ع) the First Ulul-'Azm Prophet

The Holy Quran speaks of Nuh عليه السلام in many verses; altogether, this great Prophet is mentioned in twenty-nine Surahs of the Quran, and his name appears 43 times.

The Holy Quran describes various parts of his life in detail—parts that mostly relate to aspects of teaching, education, and taking heed.

Historians and commentators have written that Nuh's عليه السلام name was "Abdul-Ghaffar," "Abdul-Malik," or "Abdul-A'la," and the title "Nuh" was given to him because for many years—

—he lamented (nuhah) for himself or for his people. His father's name was "Lamik" or "Lamech." There is disagreement regarding his lifespan; some say 1490 years, and in some narrations, 2500 years is mentioned. Regarding his people, long lifespans of around 300 years have been recorded. What is certain is that he had a very long life, and according to the explicit text of the Quran, he remained among his people for 950 years (engaged in preaching).

Nuh had three sons: "Ham," "Shem," and "Japheth." Historians believe that all current races on Earth trace back to these three sons: one group is the "Hamitic" race residing in Africa, another is the "Semitic" race residing in the Middle East and Near East, and the "Japhetic" race is considered to be the inhabitants of China.

There is also discussion regarding how many years Nuh lived after the Flood; some have written 50 years, and others 60 years.

In Jewish sources (the current Torah), there is also a detailed discussion about the life of Nuh, which differs from the Quran in many respects and is among the signs of the Torah's distortion. These discussions are mentioned in chapters 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 of the Book of Genesis.

Nuh had another son named "Canaan" who opposed his father and was not even willing to join him in the Ark of Salvation; he sat with the wicked and lost the value of the household of Prophethood, and according to the explicit text of the Quran, he eventually drowned in the Flood like the other disbelievers.

There is also discussion about how many people believed in Nuh during this long period and boarded the Ark with him: some have written 80 people and others 7! The story of Nuh has a vast reflection in Arabic and Persian literature, mostly focusing on the Flood and his Ark of Salvation. ^1 Nuh was an icon of patience, gratitude, and resistance. It is said he was the first person who, to guide humanity, utilized the logic of reasoning and intellect in addition to the logic of revelation (as is well reflected in the verses of this Surah), and for this reason, he holds a great right over all the monotheists of the world.

We conclude the biography of Nuh with a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: He said: Nuh used to recite this prayer and supplication at evening and morning:

"I have entered the evening bearing witness that whatever blessing I have in religion or worldly life, it is from Allah alone, who has no partner; to Him belongs praise for them and much gratitude." Then Allah revealed: "Indeed, he was a grateful servant" (17:3), and this was his gratitude. [^2]

  1. Regarding the phrase "My Lord! Forgive me, and my parents, and whoever enters my house as a believer," there is discussion about the meaning of "house" (bayt). Altogether, four meanings have been mentioned for it: some—

^1: A'lam al-Quran, Farhang-e Qisas-e Quran, Dehkhoda Encyclopedia under "Nuh," and Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 11. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 11, p. 291, Hadith 3.

—have considered it to mean his personal house, some a mosque, some the Ark of Nuh, and some his religion and Sharia. In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "The meaning of 'house' here is Wilayah (Guardianship); whoever enters the Wilayah has entered the house of the Prophets عليهم السلام."

"Whoever enters the Wilayah has entered the house of the Prophets عليهم السلام." ^1

O Allah! Grant us the success to enter the house of the Prophets through the acceptance of the Wilayah of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt. O Lord! Grant us such steadfastness that, like the great Prophets such as Nuh, we do not grow weary in the path of inviting to Your religion and never cease our efforts. O God! When the storm of Your wrath and anger arrives, deliver us by the Ark of Salvation of Your grace and mercy.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Nuh. 1st of Muharram al-Haram, 1407.

^1: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 429.
